Flextronics identified three critical capabilities necessary for success: Leadership, Customer Connectivity, and Efficiency. The HR organization (using a “Discover, Design, Deliver” model) was subsequently built to strategically support these three specific deliverables.

Intel determined that to succeed, they needed to focus on enabling fast innovation, brilliant talent, and proactive collaboration. Their HR transformation involved downgrading non-strategic HR practices to free up resources to focus on these three core capabilities.

Takeda North America (TPNA) identified Talent, Accountability, and Innovation as the most critical capabilities needed to maintain rapid growth and compete in the pharmaceutical industry. The subsequent redesign of the HR department, including the creation of a dedicated Talent Management Group, was based entirely on delivering these capabilities.



22.11.4 4. Capabilities as the Deliverables of HR
The transformation of HR practices (Phase 3) requires alignment, integration, and innovation around these capabilities. HR work must be transformed to ensure that the organization can effectively acquire and sustain the capabilities necessary to meet the strategic goals and stakeholder demands identified in Phase 1.

title: 22  HR Transformation
text:
22.12 HR Department Redesign (Structure)

22.12.1 1. Guiding Principles for HR Organizational Design
The sources identify three overriding organizational design principles for transforming the HR department structure:

Follow the Business Logic and Structure: The HR organization must reflect the logic and structure of the company it serves. This involves mirroring the level of centralization or decentralization found in the business organization.

In a holding company configuration (decentralized), HR logic and processes are found within independent business units (BUs).

In a single business or related diversification configuration (more centralized), HR logic, strategies, and practices are shared across BUs.

The danger is the “one size fits all” trap in diversified companies, where HR strategies must be customized to fit unique business requirements.


Operate as a Professional Service Organization: Like a law firm or consulting firm, the HR organization earns respect by making knowledge productive. HR rests on a body of knowledge that must be used by internal clients to improve their efficiency and effectiveness. Strategic HR work done well should make achieving business results easier for business leaders.

Differentiate Strategic and Transactional Work: Separating strategic (transformational) work from transactional (administrative) work is paramount.

Strategic work, guided by Phase 2 outcomes, focuses on activities with the most business impact.

Transactional work must be done efficiently.

If transactional work is not done flawlessly, HR organizations lack credibility when attempting to play strategic roles. The Pfizer case study explicitly highlights the necessity of separating transactional from transformational work (Chapter 10, Tool 4.3).




22.12.2 2. The Five Channels of the HR Organization
Building on these principles, the sources propose that the HR organization can be structured into five distinct, overlapping channels of responsibility:









Channel
Focus/Role (Function)
Strategic/Transactional
Case Study Examples




1. Service Centers
Deliver basic HR administration and transactions efficiently (e.g., payroll, benefits, applicant tracking).
Transactional/Administrative
Implemented via technology (self-service) or outsourcing.


2. Corporate HR (HR Oversight)
Sets corporate culture/identity, guides the CEO’s agenda, ensures alignment, arbitrates disputes, and manages HR professional development.
Strategic/Oversight
Very small HR leadership team kept at TPNA (Takeda).


3. Embedded HR (Strategic Business Partnering)
Works directly with line managers to clarify strategy, perform organizational audits, manage talent, and implement appropriate HR practices for the business unit.
Strategic/Consulting
Called “HR business partners” (HRBPs) at Flextronics and Takeda.


4. Centers of Expertise (HR Expertise)
Acts as specialized internal consultants, designing best-practice HR policies and programs (menus of services) that are aligned, integrated, and innovative.
Strategic/Design
Flextronics established COEs for Total Rewards, Talent Acquisition, etc..


5. Operational Execution
Delivers and implements operational tactics, often managing individual casework, reporting, and delivery of new initiatives that fall outside the service center.
Tactical/Implementation
Managed by “junior business partners” or “HR operational consultants” to free up embedded HR professionals.





22.12.3 3. Structural Design and Implementation
The transformation process involves defining the scope and function of each channel:

Service Centers (Channel 1): Transformation here focuses on leveraging e-HR (technology-enabled self-service) or outsourcing to gain efficiency, speed, and standardization.

Embedded HR (Channel 3): These professionals are key to execution, engaging in strategy assessment, organizational audits, and selecting/implementing practices from COEs. HR must be sure to staff these roles with HR professionals who have the necessary strategic architect competency.

Operational Execution (Channel 5): This role evolved to address the failure of earlier transformations where embedded HR professionals became overwhelmed by operational casework and tactical delivery. Successfully filling this channel requires selecting individuals who are execution- and implementation-oriented and managing priorities to ensure they focus on high-priority work.

The sources emphasize that successfully redesigning the department (Milestone 9) requires not just an organization chart, but also a “rules of engagement” charter that clarifies how the different groups (Service Centers, COEs, Embedded HR, etc.) will collaborate and communicate to serve stakeholders.


22.12.4 4. Case Study Examples of Structural Redesign
The case studies illustrate the application of this organizational model:

Flextronics adopted a “Discover, Design, Deliver” model:

Discover (HR Business Partners): Embedded in business segments to drive strategic HR focus.

Design (Centers of Excellence): Established COEs for specialized areas like Total Rewards and Talent Acquisition.

Deliver (HR Operations): Handled core programs and regional implementation, supported by shared services implementation in Asia.


Pfizer created three primary structures to address its redundancy and inefficiency issues:

Enterprise (Centers of Expertise): Focused on work impacting the entire company (e.g., Compensation and Benefits philosophy).

Business Unit (Embedded HR): Focused on organizational and leadership effectiveness within the BU.

Manager and Operations Support (MOS): Centralized foundational/transactional work to support sites efficiently, effectively replacing the generalist role.


Takeda North America (TPNA) structured its department to include a Talent Management center of excellence, an Employee Experience Group (operational HR), and HR business partners. They successfully used an in-sourced Employee Resource Center (ERC) to absorb tactical work and free up HR generalists for strategic roles.

title: 22  HR Transformation
text:
22.13 HR Practice Transformation (Work Flows)

22.13.1 1. Content: The Four Domains of HR Work (Flows)
The sources synthesize the vast array of HR work into four fundamental flows or domains of practice that are central to organizational success. Transformation is limited if practices are narrowly defined or addressed in isolation.








Flow of Work (Domain)
Description and Purpose
HR Professional Involvement




People Practices
Focuses on the flow of people—how talent moves into, through, up, and out of the organization. It involves ensuring the availability and development of competent, committed, and contributing talent.
HR professionals create choices in areas like recruiting, training, promotions, and retention (“Six Bs”) to upgrade talent.


Performance Practices
Focuses on the flow of performance management, linking people to work via standards, measures, rewards, and feedback. It promotes accountability by defining, noting, and rewarding performance.
HR professionals help clarify strategy, set measurable standards, design financial/nonfinancial rewards, and provide follow-up feedback (chat, data, explanation).


Information Practices
Focuses on the flow of information, ensuring people have the requisite internal and external data (customer, shareholder, economic) to perform their work.
HR professionals, sensitive to people and processes, assist in managing communication strategies (message, voice, recipient, timing, medium) to share consistent messages.


Work Practices
Focuses on the flow of work: who does the work, how it is done, and how organizational structures integrate efforts into a whole. This includes organizational design, process improvements, governance, and physical setting.
HR professionals assist in organizational design aligned with business strategy, facilitating process reengineering (reducing low-value work), and advising on decision-making governance.





22.13.2 2. Process: Three Ways to Transform HR Practices
To ensure maximum impact, HR transformation must enhance practices in three ways: Align, Integrate, and Innovate.

22.13.2.1 A. Align with Strategy

Focus on Capabilities: Practices must align with the organization’s overall strategy and, specifically, the organizational capabilities required to execute that strategy (Phase 2 outcomes). If an HR practice does not clearly link to capabilities and strategy, it should be eliminated or changed.

Customer and Investor Criteria: HR practices must be tested against external demands. For example, performance management standards should be tested with customers or investors to confirm that meeting those standards increases the likelihood of selling products or securing investment.



22.13.2.2 B. Integrate with Each Other

Unified Focus: HR practices must work together with a unified strategic focus to achieve the same outcomes. Misalignment occurs, for instance, if employees are hired for skills A, B, and C, but trained for D, E, and F, and rewarded for G, H, and I.

Synergy: Integration ensures that the results of HR work are based on synergy, meaning “the whole must be more than the parts”. Practices like performance management, talent practices, compensation, and communication must present a consistent point of view.

Overcoming the Midrange Barrier: Incremental improvements often yield little return in business results. HR leaders need the resilience to develop an entire system of strategic HR practices, pushing performance past a threshold (estimated around 75 or 80 on a 100-point scale) where significant business impact occurs.



22.13.2.3 C. Innovate

Continuous Improvement: HR professionals must keep abreast of the most recent HR practice innovations both within their industry and in the broader HR community.

Adaptation over Adoption: Innovations should be examined against the specific organizational capabilities being driven. If a practice enhances a capability (e.g., customer connectivity or speed), it should be adapted to fit the organization’s unique culture and goals, not merely adopted verbatim.




22.13.3 3. Making the Change Happen
Transformation requires a prioritized approach (Milestone 10).

Prioritization through Audits: HR audits are used to prioritize which practices receive the most investment of money, time, and talent. Audits can assess best practices, stakeholder perceptions, strategic alignment, and the level of integration among practices.

Blueprinting and Implementation: The outcome is a blueprint defining where to prioritize HR work. Implementation involves mapping core HR practices, creating a master systems design plan, establishing outcomes and measures, and implementing changes in concert with department and people changes.

Case Example (Intel): Intel successfully upgraded HR practices by focusing the Operations Group on reducing cost and complexity. This required downgrading many world-class practices that did not provide a competitive advantage to free up resources for more strategic capability development (fast innovation, brilliant talent, proactive collaboration).

title: 22  HR Transformation
text:
22.14 Upgrade HR Professionals (People)

22.14.1 1. The Model for Upgrading HR Professionals
The sources lay out a four-step model, adapted from general talent management, for building and sustaining the necessary competencies among HR professionals [101, 120–21, 240, 287]:

Articulate a theory or set a standard.

Assess individuals and organizations.

Invest in talent improvement.

Follow up and track competence.



22.14.2 2. Step 1: Defining Standards (Competencies, Roles, and Activities)
Defining success requires clarifying what HR professionals must know and do. This is typically done by defining roles and activities, which combine to form core competencies.

22.14.2.1 A. HR Roles
Effective HR professionals must master a range of evolving roles, ensuring they deliver value and avoid second-tier status:

Employee Advocate and Human Capital Developer: Focuses on the present employee (advocacy) and preparing employees for the future (development).

Functional Expert: Designs and delivers HR practices efficiently, encompassing technology-based administrative efficiency and policy/intervention design.

Strategic Partner: Helps line managers achieve goals by bringing business, change, consulting, and learning knowledge to the partnership. The Strategic Partner role is broad, encompassing the dimensions previously attributed to the Change Agent role.

Leader: Unifies the other four roles and includes leading the HR function, ensuring corporate governance, and monitoring the HR community.



22.14.2.2 B. HR Competencies
The sources identify six core competencies necessary for high-impact HR professionals, managing both the people (human dimensions) and the business (mastering requirements) axes:

Credible Activist: Must be both respected (credible) and offer a point of view, challenge assumptions, and take a position (active).

Culture and Change Steward: Shapes the company’s culture by translating external expectations into internal behaviors, helping the organization adapt to new conditions, and facilitating change throughout the organization.

Talent Manager and Organization Designer: Masters both talent management (how individuals move through the firm) and organizational design (embedding capabilities like collaboration or innovation into structure and process).

Strategy Architect: Provides a vision for how the organization can win, participates actively in creating strategy, and translates that strategy into HR practices that build organizational capabilities.

Operational Executor: Executes the transactional and administrative aspects of HR flawlessly (e.g., payroll, benefits, policies) through technology, shared services, or outsourcing. Flawless execution ensures HR credibility.

Business Ally: Contributes to business success by knowing the social context, understanding the company’s value chain (how it makes money, customers, competitors), and having a good understanding of internal business processes (passing a business literacy test).




22.14.3 3. Step 2: Assessment
Once standards are set, HR professionals must be assessed to identify strengths and development needs.

Informal Assessment: HR professionals should constantly seek feedback, reflect on their work (what is easy versus what is avoided), and seek suggestions for improvement.

Formal Assessment (360-Degree Feedback): Receiving formal feedback from peers, subordinates, supervisors, and clients (stakeholders) helps identify patterns in behavior and competence gaps.

Group Assessment: Organizations can use tools, such as the RBL HR Competencies 360 Assessment, to assess the entire HR community and identify pervasive skill gaps (e.g., lack of business ally competence) that require large-scale intervention.



22.14.4 4. Step 3: Investment (Hiring and Nurturing)
Investment means allocating resources and time to upgrading HR skills, which involves both nature (hiring) and nurture (development) [114–15, 272].

Hiring and Staffing: Companies must hire individuals with the required skills and must remove poorly performing HR professionals who consistently fail to meet standards. Removing poor performers is a critical strategy to upgrade the HR community and maintain credibility with line managers.

Job Experience: The most powerful way to learn is by doing. Learning opportunities include moving between specialist (Center of Expertise) and generalist (Embedded HR) roles, taking international assignments, doing cross-functional rotations (e.g., participating in a customer internship), and working in a turnaround situation.

Training Experience: Formal training must be integrated and connected directly to strategy, customers, and investors, and should focus on outcomes and application rather than simply being a discrete event (parade of stars).

Life Experience: Personal learning involves continuous reading, attending conferences, adapting best practices, trying new behaviors, and keeping a learning journal.



22.14.5 5. Step 4: Measurement and Follow-Up
This final step ensures that the transformation endures.

Tracking Results: HR leaders must measure the quality of HR professionals and the impact of the development investments using consistent 360-degree reviews to track progress against baselines.

Integration: The development initiative must be integrated into HR systems, meaning new HR professionals are hired for future skills, and HR training, performance management, compensation, and communication are designed to create the desired organizational capabilities.



22.14.6 Case Study Examples

Intel: Intel’s transformation involved shifting HR roles. Initial efforts (2001–2006) focused on bringing forth project management, process engineering, and data analytics skills (Operational Executor). The shift from tactical to strategic (2006 onward) required major changes to up-level the Business-Facing Channel, demanding analytical and consulting skills, often necessitating hiring new talent. Intel sought to balance its HR population by including professionals from traditional HR backgrounds, other analytical functions (finance, engineering), and external disciplines.

Pfizer: Pfizer recognized the need to upgrade its talent base to move beyond foundational work. The HR leadership team used competency studies to create role profiles and assessed its senior talent bench to place the strongest internal talent into key HR and MOS lead positions. This involved moving generalists into MOS roles and specialists into HR lead roles, representing a significant shift in talent deployment.

Flextronics: Since Flextronics built its strategic HR organization from scratch, it brought in new HR leaders externally to jumpstart the function. It then leveraged the HR Competency Study data to assess the “new HR” professionals needed, designing a career-acceleration training curriculum and guidebook used for selection, assessment, and development to grow future leaders internally.

Takeda North America (TPNA): TPNA redefined roles, repositioning HR generalists with the right skills as HR Business Partners who required excellent consulting skills, business acumen, and a strong financial background. The team also focused on retooling existing employees, bringing in new external talent, and transitioning out those whose skills did not match the new organizational needs.

title: 22  HR Transformation
text:
23.1 Ensure Successful Implementation

23.1.1 1. The Blueprint for Implementation: Milestones and Activities
Chapter 8, titled “Making It Happen,” summarizes the process for successful implementation through a set of milestones, activities, and outcomes that integrate all four phases of the transformation model into an action plan. Successfully ensuring implementation requires reaching these key milestones, particularly those dedicated to accountability:

Milestone 13: Make sure that the transformation team is staffed by the right mix of people and engaged in the right activities. The transformation team, established early in Phase 1, has the ongoing responsibility to direct and oversee the transformation in a timely and coherent way.

Activity: The team must develop a blueprint for the overall transformation that puts the preceding milestones into a responsibility chart. This blueprint should be regularly reviewed and updated to keep the transformation on track.

Activity: The team evaluates data from audits (organizational capability and HR audits) and creates work streams that move the transformation forward.

Activity: The team must continually communicate progress and the next steps to key stakeholders.

Outcome: A high-performing HR transformation team ensures the transformation stays on track and delivers the results declared in Phase 2.




23.1.2 2. Turning Content into Reality: Conditions for Successful Change
Beyond monitoring milestones, successful implementation requires actively managing the process of change. The sources identify seven key factors that help turn “what should be done” (the blueprint) into “what is actually done” (successful reality). The HR transformation team can adapt these seven conditions to make implementation happen:







Condition for Successful Change
Implication for HR Transformation




Leading
Gaining leadership support for the HR transformation from both line management and HR.


Creating a Shared Need
Building a case for why HR transformation adds value by maintaining dissatisfaction with the status quo and a compelling vision of the future.


Envisioning
Having a model of what HR transformation includes (practices, functions, people) and continually emphasizing the Phase 1 (Why) and Phase 2 (Outcomes) results to reinforce the purpose.


Engaging
Mobilizing commitment from key individuals (getting buy-in from everyone, line and HR). This involves building on supporters and focusing on the middle 60% of employees.


Decision Making
Starting small by focusing on early decisions and applying a clear decision protocol (clarity, responsibility, timeline, process, and follow-up) to move the transformation forward.


Institutionalizing
Weaving the HR transformation into all HR practices and resource allocation systems in the company to ensure it lasts over time.


Monitoring and Learning
Tracking the success of the transformation, refining and adjusting the blueprint, and learning from the effort [348, 362–363].





23.1.3 3. Institutionalizing and Sustaining the Change
Successful implementation is realized when the transformation is institutionalized—that is, embedded in the organization so that it lasts over time.

Integrating Work Streams: This requires treating HR department structure, practices, and people as integrated issues, not separate ones. The implementation must manage integration so that, for example, changes in the performance management system are supported by aligned training programs.

Changing the Culture: The transformation must be seen not as a one-time event, but as a new pattern of behavior and thinking. Change becomes sustained when the new behaviors are assimilated into the way work is done, creating a new culture that focuses on external customer and investor expectations.

Measurement and Follow-Up: Institutionalizing means that the new HR direction is embedded in HR systems. This requires:

Hiring new HR professionals who possess the skills for future expectations.

Designing and delivering HR training, performance management, compensation, and communication systems to create the desired organizational capabilities (Phase 2 outcomes).

Measuring progress using leading indicators (behaviors) and lag indicators (outcomes) to identify early signs of success or failure.


The ability of HR to achieve these goals moves them into full partnership with the business.

title: 23  Redesigning Work
text:
23.1 Executive Summary
This document synthesizes the core arguments and frameworks from Lynda Gratton’s Redesigning Work, which posits that the global pandemic created a once-in-a-century opportunity to fundamentally rethink and reshape working practices. The central thesis is that organizations, having been thrust into a massive “unfreeze” state as described by psychologist Kurt Lewin, now have a unique chance to be bold and intentional in designing work that is more purposeful, productive, and fulfilling. The document outlines a practical, four-step design process—Understand, Reimagine, Model and Test, Act and Create—that guides organizations in developing a unique corporate “signature” rather than adopting a one-size-fits-all solution.
Key takeaways include the critical trade-offs between place and time. Reimagining place involves balancing the cooperation and serendipity of the co-located office with the energy and well-being derived from distributed homeworking. Reimagining time involves balancing the coordination benefits of synchronous, connected work with the need for focus in asynchronous, disconnected periods. The analysis emphasizes that successful redesign is driven by a deep understanding of productivity elements, human networks, and employee needs, which are themselves shaped by profound technological, demographic, and social trends like automation and longer lifespans. Ultimately, the success of any new model depends on its ability to be future-proofed, support technological transitions, and be perceived as fair and just by all employees, a process that requires active co-creation and engaged leadership.

title: 23  Redesigning Work
text:
23.2 The Opportunity to Redesign Work
The source material frames the current moment as an unprecedented opportunity for change, driven by a global disruption that broke deeply ingrained and often dysfunctional working habits. This period of transformation offers a chance to build on key lessons learned and consciously design a better future of work.

23.2.1 A Global “Unfreeze” Moment
The book applies Kurt Lewin’s “freeze, unfreeze, refreeze” model of institutional change to describe the impact of the pandemic.

Freeze State: Before 2020, most organizations were in a “frozen” state, with fixed cultures, structures, and processes. While some companies were beginning to experiment with flexibility in response to automation and demographic shifts, change was slow and small-scale.

Unfreeze State: The pandemic initiated a global “unfreeze” of unimaginable scale and velocity. It forced mass remote work, dismantling bureaucracy and challenging long-held assumptions about where and when work could be done. This created a collective experience of experimentation and imagination.

The Challenge: The current challenge is to leverage this “unfreeze” state to create lasting, positive change before organizations “refreeze” into new, potentially suboptimal habits. Leanne Cutts, former Group CMO at HSBC, described this stage as working with “wet clay,” emphasizing the need for imagination to “raise the bar.”



23.2.2 Key Lessons from the Pandemic Experience
The collective experience of the pandemic yielded several critical insights that form the foundation for redesigning work:

Accelerated Digital Skills: The workforce adopted new digital tools and skills with unprecedented speed, proving that rapid learning is possible.

Bureaucracy on the “Bonfire”: Organizations became more agile, shedding unnecessary processes and prioritizing ruthlessly. As Stanford professor Nicholas Bloom described it, this was “a bonfire of unnecessary regulations.”

True Flexibility is Possible: The period demonstrated that flexible work is viable for many roles, opening up opportunities to attract diverse talent and improve work-life integration.

The “Off” Switch is Critical: The shift to remote work highlighted the danger of an “always on” culture, which leads to longer hours and burnout. Data showed the average workday expanded by nearly 49 minutes.

Human Connections Matter: The experience exposed the vital, often-unseen role of human networks. While strong ties with close colleagues were maintained, the weaker ties that fuel serendipity and innovation eroded, particularly impacting the socialization of young employees.

title: 23  Redesigning Work
section: 23.3 A Four-Step Process for Redesigning Work
text:
23.3 A Four-Step Process for Redesigning Work
The book proposes a structured, four-step design process to help organizations create a unique work model—their corporate “signature”—that aligns with their specific purpose, values, and capabilities.

Understand: Build a deep foundation by analyzing what truly matters for the business. This includes identifying the jobs and skills crucial for productivity, understanding how knowledge flows through human networks, and discovering what employees want and need from work.

Reimagine: Use the foundation of understanding to creatively devise optimal work designs. This involves exploring the choices and trade-offs of place (office vs. home) and time (synchronous vs. asynchronous) to enhance productivity and well-being.

Model and Test: Test the reimagined ideas against potential risks and key factors. A successful model must be future-proofed against demographic shifts, supportive of technological transitions, and perceived as equitable and fair by all employees.

Act and Create: Embed the new model into the company’s practices and culture. This requires engaging leaders to shape the narrative, supporting the pivotal role of managers, and implementing co-creation processes that involve employees in the change.

title: 23  Redesigning Work
text:
23.4 Step 1: Understand
A successful redesign must be built on a deep, evidence-based understanding of the organization’s unique context. This involves analyzing productivity, networks, people, and the overall employee experience.

23.4.1 The Four Elements of Productivity
Productivity in knowledge work is multifaceted. Deconstructing jobs into their core tasks reveals four primary elements:

Energy: The vitality needed for dynamic tasks, such as managing high-pressure projects or engaging in intense meetings.

Focus: The deep concentration required for tasks like writing, data analysis, or strategic thinking, which demands a rested brain free from distractions.

Coordination: The efficient alignment with others on shared goals, including check-ins, feedback, and project management.

Cooperation: The participative sharing of ideas to generate new solutions and drive innovation, often in cross-functional teams.



23.4.2 Human Networks and Knowledge Flow
Work is embedded in networks of human connection through which knowledge and innovation flow. Any redesign must consider its impact on these networks.

Tacit vs. Explicit Knowledge: Explicit knowledge is easily codified (e.g., in manuals), while tacit knowledge (know-how, mental models) resides within trusted relationships and is harder to transfer.

Strong vs. Weak Ties:

Strong Ties: Relationships with trusted colleagues are excellent for exploiting existing tacit knowledge within a team. Proximity is a significant driver of their formation.

Weak Ties: Acquaintances and broader connections are crucial for distributing new information and creating “serendipitous” encounters that spark innovation. These were the connections most damaged by mass remote work.


Boundary Spanners: Individuals who connect otherwise separate networks play a vital role in synthesizing diverse knowledge and fostering exploration.



23.4.3 What People Want: Present and Future Needs
Understanding employee needs is critical. This can be achieved by creating “personas” (e.g., Julio, the experienced manager who loves remote work, and Mandy, the young new joiner who needs the office) and by analyzing universal trends shaping future aspirations.

To Stay Healthy: With people living longer, maintaining physical and mental health becomes paramount. Employees will seek work that allows for sufficient sleep, exercise, and social connection.

To Have a Balanced Family Life: The rise of dual-career couples and diverse family structures means employees need flexibility to manage complex personal lives.

To Learn (Faster Than Machines): The relentless pace of automation creates anxiety but also a powerful motivation to upskill and reskill. Organizations that support learning will attract and retain talent.

To Live a Multistage Life: The traditional three-stage life (education, work, retirement) is being replaced by a more flexible, multistage life where people may take sabbaticals, start side businesses, or re-enter education at various ages.



23.4.4 The Employee Experience of Work
The employee journey must be analyzed to understand “pain points” and “gain points.”

Case Study (HSBC): The global bank applied marketing tools to understand the employee experience. A multidisciplinary team mapped the employee journey, using data analytics and an Employee Net Promoter Score (eNPS) to identify and resolve frustrations, such as the crucial transition between graduate placements. The goal is to create an employee experience that is personalized, intuitive, and frictionless, mirroring modern consumer technology platforms.

title: 23  Redesigning Work
text:
23.5 Step 2: Reimagine
Based on a deep understanding, organizations can begin to reimagine work by making intentional choices about place and time, aiming to maximize upsides while mitigating inherent trade-offs.

23.5.1 The Core Choices of Place and Time
The fundamental design choices revolve around two axes, each with distinct benefits and drawbacks.










Axis
Dimension
Description
Upside (+)
Downside (-)




Place
Co-located (e.g., office)
Working together physically
Boosts Cooperation through proximity and serendipity.
Depletes Energy due to commutes and office distractions.



Distributed (e.g., home)
Working from separate locations
Boosts Energy by eliminating commutes and allowing for healthier routines.
Reduces Cooperation by shrinking networks and chance encounters.


Time
Synchronous
Working and connected at the same time
Boosts Coordination by making real-time alignment easier.
Depletes Focus due to interruptions and an “always on” culture.



Asynchronous
Working disconnected from others
Boosts Focus by allowing for deep, uninterrupted concentration.
Reduces Coordination as real-time collaboration becomes difficult.



The goal is to design work that optimizes for each element: the office for cooperation, the home for energy, asynchronous time for focus, and synchronous time for coordination.


23.5.2 Reimagining Place

The Office as a Place of Cooperation

Case Study (Fujitsu): In Japan, Fujitsu reimagined the office as a typology of spaces designed for different tasks:

Neighbourhood Shared Office: Quiet, local cubicles for individual focus.

Satellite Office: Suburban spaces for project teams to meet for coordination.

Hub Office: Exciting city-center spaces designed to maximize cooperation and innovation.


Case Study (Arup): The design firm intentionally uses its Melbourne office to foster cooperation by playing with proximity (moving teams quarterly), reducing personal space to create collaborative zones, and acting as a community “node” that welcomes partners and hosts industry events.


The Home as a Source of Energy

Boundary Management: To be energizing, homeworking requires intentional “boundary work”—creating physical, cognitive, and temporal separation between work and personal life to prevent role spillover and distractions.

Case Study (BT & TCS): Veteran remote-work companies emphasize the need to manage performance by focusing on outcomes, not presenteeism. This builds trust and alleviates anxiety that remote workers will be overlooked for promotion.

Key Considerations: The redesign must address the deep gender dynamics of homeworking (where women often shoulder more domestic labor) and the complex impact on sustainability (where home energy use can offset commute savings).




23.5.3 Reimagining Time

Asynchronous Time for Focus

Human focus, unlike machine processing, is essential for future-oriented, imaginative, and creative tasks. It requires a rested brain and is compromised by multitasking.

Strategies: The redesign should include scheduling protected blocks of quiet time, promoting practices that support adequate sleep, and encouraging leaders to role-model unplugging to combat the “always on” culture.


Synchronous Time for Coordination

While vital, synchronous time is often overused, leading to meeting overload.

A Rhythm of Coordination: The most productive teams are not always connected but interact intermittently. Periods of “silence” allow for individual thought, while periods of “sound” allow for the pooling and synthesis of diverse ideas.

Case Study (TCS): The tech firm creates a rhythm through agile rituals like short, daily virtual “stand-ups” for immediate priorities and “retrospectives” at project ends to capture learnings.

Case Study (Telstra): The telecom company reinvented management by splitting the role into a “Leader of Work” (who manages scheduling and resources) and a “Leader of People” (who focuses on coaching and development), recognizing scheduling as a specialized skill.

title: 23  Redesigning Work
text:
23.6 Step 3: Model and Test
Any new work design must be tested against three critical factors: its long-term viability, its alignment with technological change, and its fundamental fairness.

23.6.1 Future-Proofing the Redesign
The model must be resilient to future demographic shifts and evolving employee aspirations.

Demographics: An aging and shrinking workforce in developed nations will intensify the war for talent, while a young and growing workforce in countries like Nigeria will create new talent pools. Models must support older workers and tap into global talent.

The Multistage Life: The model should accommodate the shift away from a linear three-stage life to a more flexible, multistage one by offering options beyond full-time, permanent employment.

Case Studies on Flexibility:

Unilever: Created “U-Work,” a “third way” employment model offering a retainer plus project pay, pension, and learning access, allowing the company to retain talent that desires more flexibility.

Open Banking: Utilized job-sharing for senior roles to retain top talent, improve work-life balance, and benefit from dual perspectives, which studies show can increase productivity.

CPP Investments: Pursuing a “golden model” to retain talent long-term, the firm offers sabbaticals and a “work from anywhere for three months” policy, aligning with its long-term investment purpose.




23.6.2 Supporting Technology Transitions
The model must facilitate the evolution of work alongside automation and AI.

Automation Pathways: Jobs will be affected differently by technology, with routine tasks facing substitution and non-routine analytical tasks seeing augmentation. Organizations must understand these pathways to guide employee development.

Case Study (NSW Public Commission): The Australian public service used data analytics to predict which of its 400,000 jobs would be automated, augmented, or newly created, allowing it to strategically realign its workforce around the customer journey and support regional development.

Building Human Skills: As machines handle routine tasks, uniquely human skills like empathy, context sensing, collaboration, and creative thinking become more valuable. The work environment must be low-stress to allow these skills to be developed and used.

Making Development Visible:

Case Study (IBM): The “Myca” chatbot helps employees see “escalator jobs”—roles that serve as gateways to higher-wage positions—and identifies the skills needed to make the transition.

Case Study (TCS): The “Knome” platform allows employees to get certified with digital “badges” for their skills and provides analytics on their internal network connections, making their collaborative capital visible.

Case Study (Microsoft): The company extends skill development into the community by partnering with local colleges to create data center academies, building a future talent pipeline in underserved areas.




23.6.3 Ensuring Fairness and Justice
For the redesign to succeed, it must be perceived as fair by all employees.

The Three Factors of Fairness:

Distributive Fairness: Are the outcomes (e.g., flexibility, workload) equitable?

Procedural Fairness: Was the process for making decisions consistent, unbiased, and representative?

Interactional Fairness: Were people treated with respect, politeness, and honesty by their managers?


Addressing Inequity: The model must proactively address potential unfairness for different groups, such as primary carers, young workers, and employees whose roles do not permit remote work.

Case Study (Brit Insurance): To refresh its values and ways of working, the insurer created a design community by randomly selecting 10% of its workforce, from receptionists to senior underwriters. This co-creation process ensured procedural fairness, built deep knowledge across the firm, and generated employee-led ideas that had strong executive backing.

title: 23  Redesigning Work
text:
23.7 Step 4: Act and Create
The final step is to translate the tested model into reality by engaging managers, employees, and leaders.

23.7.1 The Pivotal Role of Managers
Good managers are the critical link in implementing the redesign, but they are often overloaded and under-supported. Success requires reinventing their role.

Managerial Mindset Shifts: Effective managers shift from being hierarchical controllers to team enablers, from hoarding to sharing talent, from managing intact teams to orchestrating fluid project work, and from overseeing presence to coaching for outcomes.

Case Study (Telstra): By splitting the manager’s job into “Leader of Work” and “Leader of People,” the company created space for dedicated coaching and development while ensuring work itself was expertly managed.

Case Study (Standard Chartered): The bank invested heavily in its 14,000 “People Leaders,” creating a community, providing development, and building a deep coaching culture by accrediting managers to coach their peers.



23.7.2 The Power of Co-Creation
Engaging employees in the design process builds buy-in and harnesses collective intelligence, proving more effective than top-down mandates or “burning platform” approaches.

Case Study (Ericsson): The tech multinational invited all 95,000 employees to a 75-hour moderated virtual conversation about company values and future work arrangements. This massive co-creation exercise democratized the conversation, built empathy across the organization, and surfaced grassroots experiments and champions for change.



23.7.3 The Leader’s Narrative
In times of uncertainty, the stories leaders tell are pivotal for creating meaning and direction.

The Nature of Narratives: Compelling narratives engage emotions, build connections, and help people visualize an achievable future. They are more powerful than data and facts alone.

Effective Narratives: Instead of issuing binary, final edicts, the best leaders frame the redesign as an experimental journey aligned with core values, acknowledging uncertainty and inviting co-creation.

Role-Modeling: The credibility of the narrative is tested by a leader’s everyday actions. Leaders must visibly model the flexibility, collaboration, and experimental mindset they wish to foster.

title: 23  Redesigning Work
section: 23.8 Case Study in Practice: The Sage Redesign Journey
text:
23.8 Case Study in Practice: The Sage Redesign Journey
The accounting-software company Sage provides a holistic example of the four-step process in action.

Understand: The design team started with a data-driven approach, using employee surveys and performance data to build a case for change that aligned with the company’s strategic shift to a cloud-based model.

Reimagine: They co-created with 200 employees to explore future work possibilities, leading to the establishment of four guiding principles: customer-centricity, fairness and trust, human connection, and experimentation.

Model and Test: They developed four work archetypes (homeworker, flex worker, field worker, office worker) and boldly experimented with policies like a “work away” option allowing employees to work from another location for up to ten weeks.

Act and Create: They created a managerial “playbook” to guide implementation, developed talking points to ensure leadership narratives were consistent, and empowered managers to create bespoke team-level agreements, all within a framework of continuous learning and iteration.

title: 24  Blitz Scaling
text:
24.1 Executive Summary
This document synthesizes the core principles of “Blitzscaling,” a strategic framework for achieving dominant, world-changing scale at extreme speed. Blitzscaling is defined as the prioritization of speed over efficiency in an environment of uncertainty. It is a high-risk, high-reward strategy essential for navigating the “winner-take-all” or “winner-take-most” markets created by modern network effects. The central thesis is that in today’s networked age, the greatest risk is often moving too slowly, allowing a competitor to achieve critical scale and establish an insurmountable market advantage first.
The framework is built upon three core pillars of innovation:
While exemplified by Silicon Valley tech giants like Google, Amazon, and Facebook, the principles of blitzscaling are universally applicable. They have been successfully deployed in diverse industries such as fast fashion (Zara) and energy (Chesapeake Energy), within large corporations, and in non-business contexts like political campaigns (Obama 2008) and non-profits. The document concludes by underscoring the necessity of “Responsible Blitzscaling,” urging leaders to integrate ethical considerations and societal impact into their growth strategies to ensure long-term sustainability and positive influence.

title: 24  Blitz Scaling
text:
Business Model Innovation: The foundation of blitzscaling is designing a business model with inherent characteristics for exponential growth. This involves maximizing four key growth factors—Market Size, Distribution, High Gross Margins, and Network Effects—while actively designing to overcome two primary growth limiters: a lack of Product/Market Fit and Operational Scalability. Successful models often follow proven patterns like platforms, freemium services, and marketplaces.

Strategy Innovation: This involves the critical decisions of when to start and stop blitzscaling. It is an offensive strategy to seize a first-scaler advantage and a defensive one to outpace competitors. The strategy is iterative, requiring different approaches and a changing role for the founder across five distinct stages of organizational growth, categorized as Family (1-9 employees), Tribe (10s), Village (100s), City (1000s), and Nation (10,000s).

Management Innovation: Hypergrowth places extreme stress on an organization, necessitating unconventional management techniques. Leaders must navigate eight key transitions—such as moving from generalists to specialists and from being “pirates” to a “navy”—and embrace nine counterintuitive rules, including tolerating “bad” management, launching imperfect products, and letting non-critical “fires” burn to maintain focus and velocity.

title: 24  Blitz Scaling
section: 24.2 Defining Blitzscaling: The Framework for Hypergrowth
text:
24.2 Defining Blitzscaling: The Framework for Hypergrowth
Blitzscaling is a specific set of strategies and tactics designed to facilitate extreme, rapid growth by prioritizing speed over efficiency, even in the face of significant uncertainty. It is an accelerator that allows a company to grow at a furious pace, overwhelming and eliminating competition. This approach is distinct from other forms of business growth and is particularly suited to the modern economic landscape defined by the internet and network effects.
In the words of Bill Gates, “prioritizing agility in detriment of efficacy — even in midst of uncertainty —, is especially important when your business model depends on many members and obtaining their feedback. If you start soon and get that feedback, and your competitors don’t, you are on the path to success.”

24.2.1 The Four Types of Scaling
Blitzscaling can be best understood by comparing it to other forms of growth, categorized by the trade-offs between speed and efficiency under conditions of either certainty or uncertainty.









Efficiency (Prioritized)
Speed (Prioritized)




Uncertainty
Classic Startup Growth: Efficiently using resources to find product/market fit while minimizing burn rate. It is the phase of “mounting an airplane on the way down.”
Blitzscaling: Willingly sacrificing efficiency for speed without certainty of the outcome. It’s akin to attaching jet engines to the plane while still building the wings.


Certeza
Classic Scale-up Growth: Efficiently scaling a proven business model in a stable market to maximize ROI. This reflects classic corporate management techniques.
Fastscaling: Sacrificing efficiency for growth in a known environment with predictable costs. A common strategy to gain market share or hit revenue targets.



The canonical path for many successful tech companies like Google and Facebook follows a sequence: Classic Startup Growth to establish product/market fit, then Blitzscaling to achieve market dominance, followed by a transition to Fastscaling and eventually Classic Scale-up Growth as the company matures and leads its industry.


24.2.2 The Three Basic Principles of Blitzscaling

Blitzscaling is Both an Offensive and Defensive Strategy: Offensively, it allows a company to surprise the market, build a commanding lead, and attract capital and talent more easily than competitors. Defensively, it sets a pace that forces competitors into a reactive posture, denying them the time and space to execute their own differentiated strategies.

It Relies on Positive Feedback Loops: The “first-scaler advantage” is the core mechanism. Once a company reaches critical mass, it attracts the best talent and the most capital, creating a virtuous cycle. The best professionals want to work for the market leader, and investors prefer to back a clear winner. This dynamic makes it exceedingly difficult for later entrants to compete. The competition between Twitter and Tumblr illustrates this; while both were innovative, Twitter’s access to the deeper talent and capital networks of Silicon Valley allowed it to scale its executive team and outpace its New York-based rival.

It Carries Massive Risks: The pursuit of extreme growth creates significant challenges. As Mark Zuckerberg noted, “We got to a point where it took more time to fix the mistakes and problems we were creating than we gained in speed by moving forward.” The risks are both operational (e.g., technical infrastructure failing under load, as seen with Twitter’s “Fail Whale”) and managerial. The organization, its products, and leadership styles must be constantly reinvented to keep pace with growth.



24.2.3 The Five Stages of Blitzscaling
Blitzscaling is not a linear process. Each major increase in scale represents a qualitative and quantitative shift. The framework defines five stages based on organizational size, as this is the most tangible and impactful metric of change.









Stage
Name
Employee Count
Key Characteristics




Stage 1
Family
1–9
The founder personally drives growth. Communication is informal and direct. Focus is on product and finding initial users.


Stage 2
Tribe
10s
The founder transitions to managing the people who drive growth. Generalists are key. Initial processes begin to form.


Stage 3
Village
100s
The founder designs the organization that drives growth. Specialists and experienced executives are hired. Formal communication becomes essential.


Stage 4
City
1,000s
The founder makes high-level strategic decisions. The focus shifts to multithreading product lines and defending the market position.


Stage 5
Nation
10,000s
The founder’s role is to manage the existing scaled organization while initiating new blitzscaling efforts for new business units.

title: 24  Blitz Scaling
section: 24.3 The Three Core Techniques of Blitzscaling
text:
24.3 The Three Core Techniques of Blitzscaling
To successfully navigate the stages of growth, blitzscalers must master three distinct but interconnected areas of innovation.

24.3.1 A. Business Model Innovation
Designing an innovative business model capable of exponential growth is the most crucial technique. Successful models are architected to maximize growth drivers and minimize growth limiters.

24.3.1.1 The Four Growth Factors

Market Size: A large Total Available Market (TAM) is a prerequisite. Ideally, the market itself is also growing rapidly. Investors seek opportunities in markets worth at least $1 billion in annual sales. Companies like Uber and Airbnb succeeded by correctly predicting that their services would expand existing markets (taxis, hotels) far beyond traditional estimates. As Aaron Levie of Box noted, “Sizing the market for an innovator based on a declining market is like sizing the car industry based on how many horses there were in 1910.”

Distribution: A superior product with poor distribution will almost always lose to a good product with excellent distribution. Key distribution strategies for blitzscalers include:

Leveraging Existing Networks: Tapping into established platforms to reach users. PayPal famously leveraged eBay, and Airbnb leveraged Craigslist.

Virality: When users of a product attract more users. This can be organic (inherent in the product’s use, like LinkedIn profiles) or incentivized (offering rewards for referrals, like PayPal’s cash bonuses or Dropbox’s extra storage).


High Gross Margins: Gross margin (revenue minus cost of goods sold) is a powerful indicator of long-term unit economics. High-margin businesses (typically over 60%) have more capital available from each sale to reinvest in growth. Software businesses (“bits”) inherently have higher margins than physical goods businesses (“atoms”). Amazon, a low-margin retailer, has strategically built a high-margin business with Amazon Web Services (AWS), which in 2016 accounted for 150% of its operating income.

Network Effects: A product has network effects when its value to a user increases as more users join. This creates powerful positive feedback loops and durable competitive advantages, resulting in winner-take-all markets. There are five main categories:

Direct: Increased usage directly increases value for all (e.g., Facebook, WeChat).

Indirect: Increased usage spurs the creation of complementary goods (e.g., more Windows users attract more app developers).

Two-Sided: Increased usage by one user group increases value for another group (e.g., eBay’s buyers and sellers; Uber’s riders and drivers).

Local: Increased usage by a small, connected subset of users increases value for a user (e.g., “favorite” contacts on a mobile plan).

Compatibility and Standards: Use of one product encourages use of compatible products, establishing a standard (e.g., Microsoft Office document formats).




24.3.1.2 The Two Growth Limiters

Lack of Product/Market Fit: As Marc Andreessen defined it, this means “being in a good market with a product that can satisfy that market.” Without it, growth is impossible or prohibitively expensive. Blitzscaling before achieving product/market fit leads to “blitzfailing.”

Operational Scalability: Even with a great product in a huge market, a business can collapse if its operations cannot keep up with demand. This is limited by two factors:

Human Limitations: The complexity of managing relationships grows exponentially as team size increases arithmetically. A company must design a model that minimizes this, either through automation (WhatsApp) or effective outsourcing (Airbnb’s photographers).

Infrastructure Limitations: The technical and physical infrastructure must support the growth. Friendster’s failure was largely due to its servers being unable to handle its viral growth, leading to a slow user experience that drove users to MySpace. The rise of cloud services like AWS has significantly mitigated this limiter for many modern startups.





24.3.2 B. Strategy Innovation
The decision to blitzscale is a strategic choice that requires careful consideration of timing and tactics.

24.3.2.1 When to Start and Stop Blitzscaling
A company should initiate blitzscaling when it identifies a new, large opportunity, often created by technological change, where a first-scaler advantage can be secured. The presence of intense competition is the most common motivator, forcing a company to move quickly to avoid being outmaneuvered.
A company must stop blitzscaling when it approaches the limits of its market. Key warning signs include:

A declining growth rate relative to the market.

Worsening unit economics.

Decreasing employee productivity.

Increasing management overhead.

Continuing to blitzscale after hitting these limits leads to inefficient capital burn and internal conflict, as seen with Groupon and Twitter after their initial hypergrowth phases.


24.3.2.2 The Changing Role of the Founder
The founder’s role must evolve with each stage of growth:

Family: The founder personally drives every aspect of growth.

Tribe: The founder transitions to managing the people driving growth.

Village: The founder’s primary role is to design the organization and systems that enable scaling.

City: The founder focuses on high-level strategy and major resource allocation decisions.

Nation: The founder manages the mature organization while seeking and launching new blitzscaling initiatives.




24.3.3 C. Management Innovation
Blitzscaling imposes extreme demands on an organization, requiring leaders to adopt unconventional management practices.

24.3.3.1 The Eight Key Transitions

Small Teams to Large Teams: Moving from informal, spontaneous collaboration to formal processes, departments, and communication structures.

Generalists to Specialists: Shifting from hiring adaptable generalists who can do anything to hiring experts with deep domain knowledge required for scale.

Contributors to Managers to Executives: Differentiating between the roles of individual contributors, tactical managers (who oversee teams), and strategic executives (who lead managers and set vision).

Dialogue to Broadcasting: Evolving communication from informal, face-to-face conversations to formal, scalable methods like all-hands meetings, company-wide emails (like Brian Chesky’s Sunday emails at Airbnb), and internal broadcasts.

Inspiration to Data: Transitioning from founder intuition-based decisions to a data-driven culture with key metrics, dashboards, and dedicated analytics teams.

Single-Focus to Multithreading: Moving from a single-product focus to managing multiple product lines or business units simultaneously to sustain growth.

Pirate to Navy: Evolving from a rebellious, rule-breaking “pirate” startup focused solely on offense to a disciplined “navy” that can play defense, establish standards, and manage a large, stable fleet.

Founder to Leader: The founder must scale themselves by learning to delegate responsibility, amplify their impact with support staff (like a Chief of Staff), and improve their own skills at a rate that keeps pace with the company’s growth.



24.3.3.2 The Nine Counterintuitive Rules of Blitzscaling

Embrace the Chaos: Accept that uncertainty is a given and make decisions despite it, focusing on the ability to correct mistakes quickly.

Hire for the “Right Now,” Not the “Right Later”: Hire the people you need for your current stage of growth, not the person you think you’ll need in two years. The skills required for each stage are drastically different.

Tolerate “Bad” Management: In the race for speed, perfect management processes are a luxury. It’s often necessary to promote people before they are ready and accept a degree of organizational chaos to maintain velocity.

Launch a Product that Embarrasses You: If you’re not embarrassed by your first product release, you’ve launched too late. Getting to market quickly provides invaluable feedback that is superior to internal perfectionism.

Let Fires Burn: With limited resources, you must triage problems and intentionally ignore the less critical ones (the “fires”) to focus on those that threaten the company’s existence or core growth engine.

Do Things That Don’t Scale: Many crucial early-stage tasks are manual and labor-intensive (like the Airbnb founders personally photographing apartments). These are necessary to get the “engine” started before building automated, scalable solutions.

Ignore Your Customers: While counterintuitive, sometimes you must prioritize fixing a critical growth or product issue over providing perfect customer service. This is a temporary trade-off made when survival is at stake.

Raise More Money Than You Need: Extra capital provides a buffer against unforeseen problems (the “planning fallacy”) and gives you the strategic flexibility to seize opportunities without having to pause for fundraising.

Evolve Your Culture: While many fires can be left to burn, culture cannot be ignored. A strong, intentionally developed culture guides employee behavior in the absence of explicit rules and is the “connective tissue” that allows the organization to survive the rapid turnover of people and processes inherent in blitzscaling.

title: 24  Blitz Scaling
text:
24.4 The Broader Landscape of Blitzscaling
The principles of blitzscaling are not confined to Silicon Valley tech companies. They are being applied globally and across a diverse range of sectors.

Beyond High Tech: Companies like Spanish fast-fashion retailer Zara have used blitzscaling principles (prioritizing speed and responsiveness in their supply chain over cost efficiency) to achieve global dominance. Similarly, Chesapeake Energy used an aggressive land-leasing strategy to dominate the U.S. shale oil boom.

Within Large Organizations: Established companies can blitzscale new initiatives by leveraging their advantages in scale, capital, and longevity. However, they must overcome disadvantages like risk-averse incentives and shareholder pressure. A common technique is to isolate the new venture in a semi-autonomous unit, as Apple did with the Macintosh team and Google did with Android.

Beyond Business: Non-profits and political campaigns have successfully used blitzscaling. Dress for Success used a franchise-like model and partnerships to scale its services globally with limited capital. Barack Obama’s 2008 presidential campaign used internet-based distribution and community organizing tools to mobilize volunteers and donors at an unprecedented scale.

Geographic Expansion: While Silicon Valley remains a major hub, its zone of influence has expanded to include Seattle and Los Angeles, creating a “Greater Silicon Valley.” Other global hotspots like Estocolmo, London, and Tel Aviv are producing major blitzscalers.

China: The Land of Blitzscaling: China has emerged as a hyper-competitive ecosystem for blitzscaling, arguably surpassing Silicon Valley in speed and intensity. This is driven by a massive and rapidly growing market, a vast talent pool, an intense work ethic (the “996” schedule), and a willingness to fund aggressive growth. Companies like Tencent, Alibaba, and Xiaomi exemplify this trend.

title: 24  Blitz Scaling
text:
24.5 Responsible Blitzscaling
With great power comes great responsibility. As blitzscalers grow to influence the very fabric of society, they must operate ethically and consider their broader impact.

Societal Role: Companies like Facebook, Amazon, and Uber are no longer just businesses; they are critical infrastructure that shapes communication, commerce, and communities. Their actions have profound societal consequences.

The Business Case for Responsibility: Acting responsibly is good business. It builds trust with customers, helps attract and retain talent, and can preempt restrictive government regulation. A company’s success is dependent on the health of the society in which it operates.

Framework for Responsibility: Leaders can manage risk by evaluating it along two axes:

Known vs. Unknown: Differentiating between predictable risks and unforeseeable “black swan” events.

Systemic vs. Non-Systemic: Assessing whether a risk impacts only the company or has the potential to harm the entire ecosystem or society.


By consciously balancing speed with responsibility, leaders can build enduring companies that not only achieve market dominance but also contribute positively to the world.

title: 25  Scaling Up
text: 25.1 People

title: 25  Scaling Up
text:
25.1.1 The Core Mandate of the People Decision
The overarching Key Question guiding the People decision is: “Are the stakeholders (employees, customers, shareholders) happy and engaged in the business; and would you ‘rehire’ all of them?”.
This question highlights that “People” encompasses a broad range of relationships, both internal and external, which are critical to the business. Leaders must evaluate all key relationships surrounding the business, including employees, customers, shareholders, investors/bank, vendors, and advisors.
The goal of the People decision is to ensure you have the “right people doing the right things right” inside the organization. This involves identifying relationships that the company has simply outgrown—often the “toughest decisions to make”—and moving forward with necessary changes.
In the context of the larger framework, the People decision also relates to balancing the often competing Demands of the business: People (Reputation) and Process (Productivity). The aim is to continually improve the company’s Reputation with its three main groups of stakeholders: employees, customers, and shareholders. When implemented successfully, the Scaling Up tools help stakeholders, including employees and customers, “enjoy the climb”.


25.1.2 Key Components and Tools for Scaling Up People
The section dedicated to Scaling Up People focuses on three core areas: Leaders, Team, and Managers (Coaches). It introduces three foundational one-page tools:

One-Page Personal Plan (OPPP): This tool helps leaders think through their personal relationship goals. It aligns four key personal decisions (Relationships, Achievements, Rituals, and Wealth) with the four business decisions (People, Strategy, Execution, Cash), providing a “strong and fulfilled personal life… for sustaining your efforts in the business”.

Function Accountability Chart (FACe): This ensures the organization has the “right butts in the right seats” at the top, defining specific accountabilities for the company’s functions. It includes listing Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) and expected Results/Outcomes for each function. This tool helps diagnose people and performance gaps on the leadership team.

Process Accountability Chart (PACe): This delineates the four to nine key processes that drive the business and assigns accountability for each process, helping the business run smoothly. The “e” in both FACe and PACe represents the “energy and entrepreneurial spirit” necessary to scale up a business.



25.1.3 Talent Acquisition and Development
A major barrier to scaling up is Leadership, specifically “the inability to staff/grow enough leaders throughout the organization”. Therefore, attracting and retaining the right talent is critical.

Hiring: Companies should employ Topgrading methodology and use a Job Scorecard rather than a standard job description. The Job Scorecard details the job’s purpose, desired outcomes, and required competencies (cultural and technical).

A Players: When hiring, the criteria should be ranked (in order): Will, Values, Results, and Skill. Skills are considered the least important because “most skill-sets need updating every 5 years”.

Culture Fit: It is critical to hire for culture fit (alignment with Core Values) over specific skills, as cultures are like immune systems and will “spit out very capable people who don’t align with its norms”. Recruiting requires a strong marketing function to attract enough qualified applicants (ideally 20 applicants per position).

Coaching and Management: Great managers should be viewed as coaches. Managers need to focus on practices like setting clear expectations, helping people play to their strengths, giving recognition, and paying higher wages.

Education: Ongoing investment in education is critical so that “the company doesn’t outgrow them”. Training and coaching investments provide the best returns available to the business. Formal orientation (“onboarding”) is essential for inculcating new hires into the company’s culture and emphasizing Core Values and Purpose.



25.1.4 People and the Core of the Strategy
The Core of the organization—its Values, Purpose, and Competencies—provides the linkage between the People and Strategy decisions.

Core Values: These are the “handful of rules” defining the company’s culture and personality. Core Values provide a final “Should/Shouldn’t” test for all decisions. If Core Values are effectively discovered and used, they “guide all the relationship decisions and systems in the company”.

Purpose: This provides the “why” behind everything the company does (“what difference is your company making in the world?”).

Bringing the Core Alive: Leaders must align all Human Resources (People) systems and processes around the Core Values and Purpose. This includes utilizing them in recruitment, selection, onboarding, performance appraisals, recognition, and reward categories. Managers should consistently tie praise or reprimands back to a Core Value or the Purpose.

Ultimately, the strength of the People comes from having the right leadership doing the right things right (FACe), supported by the right systems and processes (PACe). Scaling Up successfully requires attracting and keeping the right people to “weather the storms” and drive growth.

title: 25  Scaling Up
text:
25.2 Strategy

25.2.1 The Key Question for Strategy
The fundamental Key Question that anchors the Strategy decision in the 4 Decisions framework is:
“Can you state your firm’s strategy simply — and is it driving sustainable growth in revenue and gross margins?”
This single question immediately ties the abstract concept of strategy to tangible, measurable financial results: sustainable growth in revenue and increasing gross margins.


25.2.2 Strategy’s Role in the Larger Framework
Strategy is one of the four key decision sets—People, Strategy, Execution, and Cash—that leaders must get “absolutely right” to successfully scale a business and maximize opportunity.
The sources emphasize that if a company does not have a “killer strategy,” it will face continuous price pressures as the market commoditizes its offerings. Furthermore, great execution is useless if the strategy is fundamentally flawed, as one leader learned, noting that great execution “won’t get you anywhere if your strategy is wrong”.


25.2.3 Answering the Key Question through Strategic Tools
The process of answering the Strategy Key Question involves using specific tools and routines designed to sharpen the company’s competitive edge.

25.2.3.1 Simplicity and Communication
A successful strategy must be simple enough to be stated clearly. This simplicity is captured in the tools used for strategic planning:

The Vision Summary: This tool provides a simplified version of the strategic plan, helping firms communicate the key aspects of the company’s vision to employees, customers, investors, and the broader community.

The One-Page Strategic Plan (OPSP): The OPSP is the key tool used to capture the entire strategic vision, demanding conciseness due to limited space, thus forcing prioritization and simplicity. The OPSP integrates various strategic concepts and is designed to drive alignment, accountability, and focus.



25.2.3.2 Driving Sustainable Growth (Financial Metrics)
The Key Question specifically measures success by sustainable top-line revenue growth and increasing gross margin dollars.

If a strategy is working, growth and margins should come “almost effortlessly”. If growth is not sustained or margins are eroding, it indicates a need to re-evaluate the strategy.

The Strategy section guides leaders to focus on gross margin dollars as the “true top line” of many firms. Increasing gross margins often requires refining the strategy to maintain sufficient differentiation and uniqueness in the offering.



25.2.3.3 Strategic Differentiation
To drive sustainable growth and increasing gross margins, the strategy must be truly differentiated. Gary Hamel’s definition is referenced, stating that a real strategy must pass two tests: first, what the company plans to do “really matters to enough customers;” and second, it differentiates the company from the competition.
The most robust framework for developing this differentiation is the 7 Strata of Strategy:

Words You Own (Mindshare): Identifying the word(s) the firm owns in the minds of its core customers.

Sandbox and Brand Promises: Defining the core customer (Who/Where), what is being sold (What), and the measurable Brand Promises the firm is making (Kept Promise Indicators).

Brand Promise Guarantee: Creating a catalytic mechanism to guarantee those promises.

One-PHRASE Strategy: Identifying the key lever that drives profitability and blocks competition by making strategic trade-offs (daring to be “bad” at some things).

Differentiating Activities (3 to 5 Hows): Defining the specific activities—how the business executes—that are difficult for rivals to copy.

X-Factor (10x–100x Underlying Advantage): Identifying a hidden, often invisible, underlying competitive advantage that completely neutralizes rivals.

Profit per X and BHAG®: Defining the overarching economic driver (Profit per X) and the measurable 10-to 25-year goal (BHAG®) that align with the strategy.

Successful strategy requires balancing the pursuit of these elements, ensuring that growth comes from a well-defined and defended niche.



25.2.4 The Strategy Routine
Answering the Key Question and developing the strategy is not a one-time event; it requires continuous strategic engagement:

Strategic Thinking vs. Execution Planning: Strategy requires separating these two activities, engaging a “council” of senior leaders to focus on strategic thinking (meeting weekly) and involving a larger team, including middle and frontline managers, for execution planning.

SWT Analysis: Senior leaders use the SWT (Strengths, Weaknesses, and Trends) analysis to look beyond industry challenges and spot major trends that could disrupt the business, helping them maintain prediction capabilities.

The Culture Connection: The Strategy discussion begins with the organization’s Core (Values, Purpose, and Competencies), which provides the foundation for an effective strategy. A weakened culture (People decision) can “torpedo any strategy”.

Repetition: The strategy elements (like the BHAG® and Purpose) must be repeatedly communicated by leadership to keep the organization aligned and heading in the same direction.



25.2.5 The Role of the Core in Strategy
A strong Core is the foundation for an effective strategy. Without it, the organization risks instability from cultural challenges, loss of focus, disengagement, and lack of heart, which can cause the business to experience the “proverbial wheels flying off as the business speeds down the highway”. Ultimately, a weakened culture (derived from the Core) will torpedo any strategy.
The Core attributes anchor the left side of the One-Page Strategic Plan (OPSP), forming the letter “C” for Core, and represent the heart and soul of the organization. The Core aligns with the “preserve the core” aspect of Jim Collins’s dual dynamic of “preserve the core/stimulate progress,” as the Core elements should hold steady over time.


25.2.6 Components of the Core
The Core consists of three main elements:

25.2.6.1 Core Values (The Soul)
Core Values are the handful of rules and boundaries that define the company’s culture and personality.

Function: Core Values provide a final “Should/Shouldn’t” test for all the behaviors and decisions made by everyone in the firm. If effectively discovered and used, these values guide all the relationship decisions and systems in the company. When values fully permeate the company, management gains the confidence to delegate tasks, trusting that employees will know the right things to do when faced with a decision or ethical dilemma.

Discovery, Not Creation: Discerning the Core Values is a DISCOVERY process, not the creation of a wish list. Trying to force a change in a firm’s Core Values is difficult and often unsuccessful, similar to trying to force a child into a mold. They just are, and the key is to articulate them accurately.

Examples: Values are best expressed as phrases, not single words (e.g., Disney’s “Preservation and control of the Disney magic”).

Alignment: Core Values should be reinforced through People (HR) systems daily. All Human Resources (HR) processes and activities (hiring, orientation, appraisal, recognition, etc.) must align with the Core Values and Purpose.



25.2.6.2 Core Purpose (The Heart)
The Core Purpose answers the “Why?”.

Function: It expresses what difference the company is making in the world and provides the meaning and passion behind everyone’s efforts. It is more heartfelt than the term “mission”.

Discovery: To discern the Purpose, a team starts by asking “What do we do?” and then repeatedly asks “Why?” (the five whys technique) until they land on the deep reason the company exists, such as Gazelles’ focus on “freedom”.

Pitfall: Teams often mistake Brand Promises (attributes of a product or service) for Purpose.

Communication: The Purpose should lead to a “stump speech” that the CEO shares repeatedly to remind everyone of the big picture and “why we do what we do”.



25.2.6.3 Core Competencies (Inherent Strengths)
Core Competencies relate to the company’s inherent strengths.

Function: Understanding inherent strengths is essential, just as understanding core weaknesses (what the company is inherently incapable of doing) is. Core Competencies provide boundaries for determining which products and services the company should pursue and help determine how to differentiate the company in the marketplace.

Attributes: According to Prahalad and Hamel, a Core Competency must have three attributes: it must not be easy for competitors to imitate; it can be reused widely for many products and markets; and it must contribute to the benefits and value the end customer experiences.

Avoid Narrowness: Leaders should avoid defining Core Competencies too narrowly; for example, BIC’s strength is “making disposable plastic anything,” not just cheap pens.




25.2.7 Bringing the Core Alive
To ensure the Core is “alive” in the organization, leaders must align all HR systems and processes around these attributes. Eight ways to reinforce the Core include:

Storytelling: Using stories or “legends” to explain the Core Values, often collected quarterly through events like a Core Values Hall of Fame.
